content:
# Genesis Chapter 17 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 9v–10r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_017_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_017_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 12–13 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209

---

**1** Now when Abram was ninety-nine years old, the LORD appeared to Abram and said to him, "I am God Almighty; walk before me and be blameless.

**2** And I will set my covenant between me and you, and will multiply you exceedingly."

**3** Then Abram fell on his face. And God spoke with him, saying,

**4** "As for me, behold, my covenant is with you, and you shall be the father of a multitude of nations.

**5** No longer shall your name be called Abram, but your name shall be Abraham, for I have made you the father of a multitude of nations.

**6** I will make you exceedingly fruitful, and I will make you into nations, and kings shall come forth from you.

**7** And I will establish my covenant between me and you and your offspring after you throughout their generations for an everlasting covenant, to be God to you and to your offspring after you.

**8** And I will give to you and to your offspring after you the land of your sojournings, all the land of Canaan, for an everlasting possession, and I will be their God."

**9** And God said to Abraham, "As for you, you shall keep my covenant, you and your offspring after you throughout their generations.

**10** This is my covenant which you shall keep, between me and you and your offspring after you: Every male among you shall be circumcised.

**11** And you shall be circumcised in the flesh of your foreskins, and it shall be a sign of the covenant between me and you.

**12** He who is eight days old among you shall be circumcised — every male throughout your generations, whether born in your house or bought with money from any foreigner who is not of your offspring.

**13** He who is born in your house and he who is bought with your money shall surely be circumcised. So shall my covenant be in your flesh for an everlasting covenant.

**14** And any uncircumcised male who is not circumcised in the flesh of his foreskin shall be cut off from his people; he has broken my covenant."

**15** And God said to Abraham, "As for Sarai your wife, you shall not call her name Sarai, for Sarah shall be her name.

**16** I will bless her, and moreover I will give you a son by her. I will bless her, and she shall be the mother of nations; kings of peoples shall come from her."

**17** Then Abraham fell on his face and laughed, and said in his heart, "Shall a child be born to a man who is a hundred years old? Shall Sarah, who is ninety years old, bear a child?"

**18** And Abraham said to God, "Oh that Ishmael might live before you!"

**19** God said, "No, but Sarah your wife shall bear you a son, and you shall call his name Isaac. And I will establish my covenant with him for an everlasting covenant for his offspring after him.

**20** As for Ishmael, I have heard you. Behold, I will bless him and make him fruitful and multiply him exceedingly. He shall father twelve princes, and I will make him into a great nation.

**21** But my covenant I will establish with Isaac, whom Sarah shall bear to you at this season next year."

**22** When he had finished speaking with him, God went up from Abraham.

**23** Then Abraham took Ishmael his son and all those born in his house and all those bought with his money — every male among the men of Abraham's household — and he circumcised the flesh of their foreskins that very day, as God had said to him.

**24** Abraham was ninety-nine years old when he was circumcised in the flesh of his foreskin.

**25** And Ishmael his son was thirteen years old when he was circumcised in the flesh of his foreskin.

**26** That very day Abraham was circumcised, and Ishmael his son.

**27** And all the men of his house, those born in the house and those bought with money from a foreigner, were circumcised with him.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Source Manuscripts

This translation was produced from direct reading of four manuscript image sources:

1. **Leningrad Codex (Codex Leningradensis, B19A)**, dated c. 1008 CE. West Semitic Research Project photographs by Bruce E. Zuckerman. Genesis 17 spans Folios 9v–10r.

   **Folio 9v** (GEN_017_page_001.jpg): This folio continues from Genesis 16, which closes at the lower portion of the right-hand column of the previous folio (Folio 9r). The transition into Genesis 17 is marked by a parasha (section) break after Genesis 16:16. The characteristic three-column layout of the Leningrad Codex is clearly legible on this folio. Genesis 17 opens in the left and center columns with the divine appearance to Abram at age ninety-nine (v. 1). The theophanic self-introduction — אֲנִי אֵל שַׁדַּי הִתְהַלֵּךְ לְפָנַי וֶהְיֵה תָמִים ("I am El Shaddai; walk before me and be blameless") — is legible in bold square script. The covenant announcement (v. 2), Abram's prostration (v. 3), and the extended divine speech of promise (vv. 4–8) extending through the name change from Abram to Abraham are readable through the middle of the folio. The circumcision commands (vv. 9–14), the name change of Sarai to Sarah (vv. 15–16), and the extended passage through verse 16 are legible. The folio appears to end at approximately verse 16, with the remainder of the chapter on the facing folio.

   **Folio 10r** (GEN_017_page_002.jpg): This folio opens with Abraham's laughter (v. 17) — the pivotal moment of the chapter's emotional core. The פ (parasha marker) for an open section is visible. The words וַיִּפֹּל אַבְרָהָם עַל פָּנָיו וַיִּצְחָק ("and Abraham fell on his face and laughed") are legible in the opening lines of the folio. Abraham's plea for Ishmael (v. 18), God's response establishing the Isaanic covenant (v. 19), the blessing of Ishmael with twelve princes (v. 20), the confirmation of the covenant with Isaac (v. 21), and the departure formula (v. 22) are all readable. The narrative of the circumcision of Abraham, Ishmael, and all the males of the household (vv. 23–27) closes the chapter with a closing parasha marker, after which Genesis 18 begins. The Masoretic notation at the foot of the folio records the verse count and other scribal data.

2. **Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209)** — Septuagint/LXX, 4th century CE. Page 12 (GEN_017_vaticanus_page_001.jpg) carries the heading at the upper margin and contains the Genesis 17 text in the Vaticanus's characteristic three-column continuous layout without verse breaks. The Genesis 17 material begins on this page in the course of the column and continues across columns. The chapter heading "CAT. ΧΙΧ" is partially visible at the right margin area (using the Vaticanus's own sequential chapter numeration). The Greek text of the theophanic introduction (Ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ θεός σου, εὐαρέστει ἐναντίον ἐμοῦ — "I am your God; be well-pleasing before me," rendering El Shaddai as ὁ θεός σου, "your God") through the circumcision commands is legible. Page 13 (GEN_017_vaticanus_page_002.jpg), headed "CAT. XX" at the right margin (indicating the transition to Genesis 18 content in the Vaticanus's own chapter system), carries the remainder of Genesis 17 — Abraham's laughter, the Isaac promise, the Ishmael blessing, and the circumcision narrative through verse 27 — before transitioning into the opening of Genesis 18.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

**1. אֵל שַׁדַּי — "God Almighty" (v. 1) — The Name El Shaddai**

The theophanic self-disclosure in verse 1 introduces one of the most significant divine names in the Pentateuch: אֵל שַׁדַּי (El Shaddai). The name occurs seven times in Genesis (17:1; 28:3; 35:11; 43:14; 48:3; 49:25), and the priestly source material characteristically uses it as the patriarchal name for God before the revelation of the Tetragrammaton (YHWH) at the burning bush — as Exodus 6:3 explicitly states: "I appeared to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob as El Shaddai, but by my name YHWH I did not make myself known to them."

The etymology of שַׁדַּי (Shaddai) is one of the most contested questions in Hebrew philology:

- **"Almighty" (traditional):** The rendering preserved in every major English tradition (KJV, ESV, NIV, NASB) traces to the LXX's παντοκράτωρ (pantokrator, "the All-Powerful, the Omnipotent") and the Vulgate's omnipotens. This rendering is semantically functional — Shaddai does operate in the texts as a name associated with divine power, particularly the power to bless — but its etymological basis in Hebrew is uncertain.

- **From שַׁד (shad, "mountain"):** A connection to the Akkadian šadû ("mountain"), yielding "God of the Mountain" or "Mountain God" — a plausible Near Eastern divine title, and one that would fit the mountain-associated theophany tradition.

- **From שַׁד (shad, "breast"):** The association with the life-sustaining, nourishing breast has been proposed on the basis that in Genesis contexts El Shaddai appears especially in contexts of fertility and blessing (Genesis 28:3 — "El Shaddai bless you and make you fruitful and multiply you"; 49:25 — "blessings of the breasts [shadayim] and of the womb"). The formal homophony with שָׁדַיִם (shadayim, "breasts") may be deliberate: El Shaddai as the God of provision, fertility, and nurture.

- **From שָׁדַד (shadad, "to destroy"):** A less favored but philologically attested derivation connecting Shaddai to destructive power.

The formal translation renders El Shaddai as "God Almighty" — maintaining the traditional rendering that is both the most familiar and the most widely accepted in the history of translation. The Notes acknowledge the etymological uncertainty without resolving it, since the Hebrew itself may deliberately exploit the polysemy. What is clear from the context is that El Shaddai here presents himself as the God with the power to do what human biology cannot: to bring covenant fruitfulness to a ninety-nine-year-old man and a ninety-year-old woman.

The Leningrad Codex is entirely clear at this point: אֲנִי אֵל שַׁדַּי (ani El Shaddai, "I am El Shaddai"). The Vaticanus LXX renders: Ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ θεός σου (Ego eimi ho theos sou, "I am your God") — a striking departure from any rendering of Shaddai, simply identifying the speaker as "your God." The Vaticanus does not render the distinctive divine name but substitutes the more general θεός with a possessive. This reflects an LXX tradition that by the time of translation did not consistently render Shaddai as a distinct name but absorbed it into the general divine title.

**2. הִתְהַלֵּךְ לְפָנַי וֶהְיֵה תָמִים — "Walk Before Me and Be Blameless" (v. 1) — The Covenant Imperative**

The two imperatives of verse 1 form the ethical counterpart to the covenant the LORD is about to announce. They are:

- **הִתְהַלֵּךְ לְפָנַי (hithalekh lefanai, "walk before me"):** The Hitpael stem of הָלַךְ (halakh, "to walk, to go") indicates continuous, reflexive, habitual movement — a sustained manner of life, not a single action. The preposition לְפָנַי (lefanai, "before me / in front of my face") means to conduct one's entire life in conscious orientation toward God's presence. This is the same language used of Enoch in Genesis 5:22, 24 (הִתְהַלֵּךְ חֲנוֹךְ אֶת הָאֱלֹהִים, "Enoch walked with God") and of Noah in Genesis 6:9 (אֶת הָאֱלֹהִים הִתְהַלֵּךְ נֹחַ, "Noah walked with God"). The preposition differs: Enoch and Noah "walked with God" (et, "with"); Abram is to "walk before me" (lefanai, "before my face"). To walk before God is to live in God's sight — a posture of accountability and transparency before the divine witness. The formal translation renders "walk before me" as the most direct rendering of לְפָנַי.

- **וֶהְיֵה תָמִים (veheyeh tamim, "and be blameless"):** The adjective תָּמִים (tamim) means "complete, whole, without blemish, blameless, perfect." It is the word used for the animals required for sacrifice — animals without physical defect (Leviticus 1:3, 10; 3:1 etc.: תָּמִים, "without blemish"). Applied to a human being, it denotes moral and relational integrity — not sinless perfection in an absolute sense, but wholeness of heart and undivided loyalty. The same word is used of Noah: נֹחַ אִישׁ צַדִּיק תָּמִים הָיָה ("Noah was a righteous man, blameless in his generation," Genesis 6:9). The formal translation renders "be blameless" — this is the standard rendering and appropriately connotes integrity of character. "Be perfect" (Vulgate: esto perfectus) is technically accurate but carries excess weight in English.

The two commands together form the ethical foundation of the covenant: walk before God (orientation) and be blameless (integrity). The covenant is not merely a divine grant; it comes with a call to covenant living.

The LXX renders: εὐαρέστει ἐναντίον ἐμοῦ καὶ γίνου ἄμεμπτος (euareste enantion emou kai ginou amemptos, "be well-pleasing before me and become blameless/without fault") — εὐαρεστέω (euaresteo, "to be pleasing, to please") for "walk before me," and ἄμεμπτος (amemptos, "without blame, blameless") for תָּמִים. The LXX substitutes "be pleasing" for the walking metaphor, losing the directional quality of "walk before me" but retaining the sense of a life lived in divine approbation.

**3. אַבְרָהָם — "Abraham" (v. 5) — The Name Change and Its Wordplay**

Verse 5 records the divine renaming of Abram to Abraham:

לֹא יִקָּרֵא עוֹד שִׁמְךָ אַבְרָם וְהָיָה שִׁמְךָ אַבְרָהָם
(lo yiqqare od shimkha Avram vehayah shimkha Avraham)
"No longer shall your name be called Abram, but your name shall be Abraham."

The etymology offered is: כִּי אַב הֲמוֹן גּוֹיִם נְתַתִּיךָ (ki av hamon goyim netattikha, "for I have made you the father of a multitude of nations"). The text presents Abraham (אַבְרָהָם) as meaning "father of a multitude" (av + hamon).

This is transparently a theological wordplay rather than a true etymological derivation. Linguistically, the name Abram (אַבְרָם) most likely derives from a common Semitic onomastic form meaning "exalted father" (ab + rum, from rum, "to be high, exalted"). The name Abraham (אַבְרָהָם) may be a dialectal variant or extended form of the same name — there is no Hebrew root אַב-הֲמוֹן (ab-hamon, "father of a multitude") that would mechanically produce the form Abraham. What the text does is provide a theological interpretation of the name: the shift from Abram to Abraham is invested with the meaning of covenant purpose — he will be the father of a multitude (hamon) of nations (goyim). The sound-change (Abram → Abraham) is made to carry the covenant promise.

The same pattern governs Sarai → Sarah (v. 15), and the birth of Isaac — whose name encodes the laughter (tsachaq) of Abraham (v. 17) and Sarah (Genesis 18:12). In each case the name is a covenant marker, a compressed theology in phonetic form.

The formal translation renders "Abraham" throughout from verse 5 onward and "Abram" in verses 1–4, following the Hebrew text's own naming progression. The LXX similarly uses Αβρααμ (Abraam) from the point of the name change.

**4. מָל בְּשַׂר עָרְלָתְכֶם — "Circumcised in the Flesh of Your Foreskins" (vv. 11, 14) — The Sign of the Covenant**

The covenant sign (אוֹת הַבְּרִית, ot habrit, "sign of the covenant") appointed in verses 10–14 is circumcision: מִילָה (milah), the removal of the foreskin (עָרְלָה, orlah). The formal translation renders all the circumcision vocabulary directly:

- **נִמַּלְתֶּם (nimmaltem, "you shall be circumcised"):** The Niphal of מוּל (mul, "to circumcise") — literally "you shall be circumcised" (passive); the formal translation renders this as "shall be circumcised."
- **בְּשַׂר עָרְלַתְכֶם (besar orlatekhem, "the flesh of your foreskins"):** The formal translation renders literally — "in the flesh of your foreskins" — preserving both nouns (flesh, foreskin) as they appear in the Hebrew.
- **אוֹת בְּרִית (ot brit, "sign of the covenant"):** Rendered "sign of the covenant" — the same structural phrase used for the rainbow in Genesis 9:12–13.

The irony noted in the manifest is significant: the verb for making a covenant is כָּרַת בְּרִית (karat brit, "to cut a covenant") — and the consequence for failing to keep the covenant sign of circumcision is that the uncircumcised male shall be כָּרַת (nikhretal, "cut off") from his people (v. 14). The verb כָּרַת (karat, "to cut") operates on both ends: the covenant is established by cutting (the name, the covenant-cutting ceremony of Genesis 15); the penalty for covenant violation is also cutting (being cut off from the community). The wordplay is inherent to the Hebrew and available to any reader of the text. The formal translation renders "shall be cut off" for the penalty, preserving the karat root.

**5. יִשְׂחַק — "Laughed" (v. 17) — Abraham's Laughter and the Name Isaac**

Verse 17 records Abraham's reaction to the promise of a son through the ninety-year-old Sarah:

וַיִּפֹּל אַבְרָהָם עַל פָּנָיו וַיִּצְחָק
(vayyippol Avraham al panav vayyitschaq)
"Then Abraham fell on his face and laughed."

The verb וַיִּצְחָק (vayyitschaq, "and he laughed") is a Qal imperfect of the root צָחַק (tsachaq, "to laugh"). The name יִצְחָק (Yitschaq, "Isaac") is directly derived from this root: the son will be named for the laughter that accompanied his announcement. The formal translation preserves "laughed" without any softening — the laughter is the text's own explanation of the name Isaac.

The question of what Abraham's laughter means has generated extensive interpretive discussion. Two primary readings:

**(a) Laughter of incredulity or disbelief:** Abraham falls on his face and laughs because the promise is biologically impossible — a hundred-year-old man and a ninety-year-old woman producing a child. He then says in his heart (v. 17) the double incredulous question, and explicitly asks God to let Ishmael be the heir (v. 18). On this reading, Abraham's faith momentarily falters; his laughter is the expression of a man for whom the promise has exceeded the range of the credible. This reading is supported by the parallelism with Sarah's laughter in Genesis 18:12–15, which God explicitly questions: "Why did Sarah laugh?" — implying that her laughter was inappropriate to the situation.

**(b) Laughter of joy or wonder:** The prostration before laughter (וַיִּפֹּל עַל פָּנָיו, "he fell on his face") is an act of worship, not of ridicule. Some interpreters read the entire sequence as Abraham's exuberant worship — he falls in adoration and laughs in wonder at a promise so extravagant it overwhelms him. On this reading, his prayer for Ishmael (v. 18) is not an alternative to the promise but a plea that Ishmael not be displaced or harmed — a father's love for his existing son alongside his wonder at the new promise.

The formal translation renders "laughed" without a qualifier, preserving the text's own ambiguity. The reader's interpretation of Abraham's laughter determines the reader's sense of his faith at this moment — and the text itself does not adjudicate.

The Vaticanus LXX renders: ἔπεσεν δὲ Αβρααμ ἐπὶ πρόσωπον αὐτοῦ καὶ ἐγέλασεν (epesen de Abraam epi prosopon autou kai egelasan, "and Abraham fell upon his face and laughed") — ἐγέλασεν (egelase, aorist of γελάω, "to laugh"), in agreement with the MT. The LXX does not soften the laughter.

**6. לוּ יִשְׁמָעֵאל יִחְיֶה לְפָנֶיךָ — "Oh That Ishmael Might Live Before You" (v. 18) — Abraham's Plea**

Abraham's intercession in verse 18 uses the particle לוּ (lu, "would that / oh that") — a particle of wishing or strong petition. The phrase לוּ יִשְׁמָעֵאל יִחְיֶה לְפָנֶיךָ ("Oh that Ishmael might live before you!") is Abraham's petition on behalf of his elder son. The verb יִחְיֶה (yichyeh, "might live / may he live") is a jussive (optative) form expressing a wish. לְפָנֶיךָ (lefanekha, "before you") is the same phrase used of Abraham himself in verse 1 (לְפָנַי, "before me") — Abraham is asking that Ishmael have the covenanted life of a man who lives in the divine presence.

This is one of the most tender moments in the Abraham narrative: the father of faith, having just received the most staggering covenant promise of his life, immediately turns to his existing son and prays for him. The formal translation renders "Oh that Ishmael might live before you!" — the particle "oh that" captures the force of לוּ as a particle of wishing rather than merely "if" (which is a possible but weaker rendering).

**7. שָׂרָה — "Sarah" (v. 15) — The Name Change of Sarai**

Verse 15 records the renaming of Sarai (שָׂרַי, Saray) to Sarah (שָׂרָה, Sarah). Both names derive from the root שָׂרָה/שָׂר (sarah/sar, "princess, noble lady, queen"). The difference between Sarai and Sarah is a final yod (י) versus a final heh (ה) — a minor vocalic change. Unlike the Abraham/Abram change, where an explicit etymology is offered (father of a multitude), no etymological explanation is given for the Sarai/Sarah change; the divine word simply announces the new name and immediately proceeds to the blessing that the new name encodes: "I will bless her, and she shall be the mother of nations; kings of peoples shall come from her" (v. 16).

The formal translation renders Sarai from Genesis 11 through Genesis 17:14, and Sarah from verse 15 onward — following the text's own naming chronology precisely.

**8. בֶּן שְׁמֹנֶה יָמִים יִמּוֹל לָכֶם — "He Who Is Eight Days Old Shall Be Circumcised" (v. 12) — The Eight-Day Rule**

The specification that circumcision is to be performed on the eighth day (בֶּן שְׁמֹנֶה יָמִים, "son of eight days," i.e., at eight days old) is one of the most discussed details in the legislation. The eighth day in the biblical calendar is consistently associated with completion and new beginning — it is the day after the full week (7 + 1 = 8), the day that begins the second cycle. The eighth day appears in Leviticus 9:1 (the eighth day of Aaron's ordination), 12:3 (the circumcision command repeated for future generations), 14:10 (the leper's eighth-day offering), and elsewhere as the day of covenant completion and entrance into a new status.

Modern medicine has noted that neonatal prothrombin levels and Vitamin K-dependent clotting factors reach peak levels around the eighth day of life, making it biologically one of the safest times for a surgical procedure on a newborn. Whether the ancient legislator knew this or whether the convergence is coincidental is a matter of discussion; the text simply commands the eighth day without explanation.

The formal translation renders "he who is eight days old among you shall be circumcised" — the Hebrew idiom בֶּן שְׁמֹנֶה יָמִים (ben shemonah yamim, "son of eight days") is an Hebraic age expression, and "who is eight days old" captures it naturally in English.

**9. וְנִכְרְתָה הַנֶּפֶשׁ הַהִוא מֵעַמֶּיהָ — "Shall Be Cut Off from His People" (v. 14) — The Karet Penalty**

The penalty for failing to keep the covenant sign is the karet (כָּרֵת) — the "cutting off" — expressed in verse 14 as:

וְנִכְרְתָה הַנֶּפֶשׁ הַהִוא מֵעַמֶּיהָ
(venikretah hanefesh hahi me'ammeha)
"And that person shall be cut off from his people."

The word נֶפֶשׁ (nefesh, "soul, person, self, life") is used here for the individual — "that soul / that person." The passive verb וְנִכְרְתָה (venikretah, "shall be cut off") is a Niphal of כָּרַת (karat, "to cut") — the same root used to describe the making of the covenant (כָּרַת בְּרִית, "to cut a covenant"). The irony is sharp: the covenant is made by cutting; the consequence of breaking the covenant is cutting off.

The exact meaning of karet — "cut off from his people" — has generated discussion in rabbinic and biblical scholarship. Options include:
- Death at the hand of the community (communal execution);
- Premature death at the hand of God (divine punishment);
- Excommunication or exclusion from the covenant community;
- Loss of eternal share in the world to come (later rabbinic interpretation).

The formal translation renders "shall be cut off from his people" — preserving the Hebrew idiom precisely. The כָּרַת / כָּרֵת wordplay is noted but cannot be replicated in English.

**10. בְּעֶצֶם הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה — "That Very Day" (vv. 23, 26) — Immediate Covenant Obedience**

The phrase בְּעֶצֶם הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה (be'etsem hayom hazeh, "in the bone/body of this day / on that very day") is a strong Hebrew idiom for immediacy and exact timing. The word עֶצֶם (etsem) means "bone" or "body/substance," and the phrase "on the very bone of this day" is an idiomatic expression for "on this exact day, on this same day, that very day." The same phrase is used in Genesis 7:13 (the exact day Noah entered the ark) and Exodus 12:41 (the exact day Israel left Egypt) — it marks moments of decisive covenant action executed with precision.

The formal translation renders "that very day" — a natural English idiom that captures the force of בְּעֶצֶם הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה. Abraham circumcises everyone in his household on the very day God commanded him — no delay, no gradual compliance, but immediate, complete obedience.

---

### LXX (Vaticanus) Variants — Genesis 17

The Vaticanus manuscript pages 12–13 (GEN_017_vaticanus_page_001.jpg and GEN_017_vaticanus_page_002.jpg) were read directly. The chapter markers "CAT. ΧΙΧ" and "CAT. XX" appear in the right-hand margins, using the Vaticanus's own sequential LXX chapter numeration. The LXX of Genesis 17 generally follows the MT closely, with the following variants of note:

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Translation Decision | Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 17:1 | אֵל שַׁדַּי ("El Shaddai / God Almighty") | ὁ θεός σου ("your God") | "God Almighty" | LXX does not render Shaddai; substitutes "your God" — a striking departure from the MT's distinctive divine name |
| 17:1 | הִתְהַלֵּךְ לְפָנַי ("walk before me") | εὐαρέστει ἐναντίον ἐμοῦ ("be well-pleasing before me") | "walk before me" | LXX renders the walking metaphor as "please" — loses the directional/spatial imagery of the Hebrew |
| 17:1 | תָמִים ("blameless") | ἄμεμπτος ("blameless, without fault") | "blameless" | Exact equivalence; ἄμεμπτος = without blame/fault |
| 17:5 | הֲמוֹן גּוֹיִם ("a multitude of nations") | πλῆθος ἐθνῶν ("a multitude/crowd of nations") | "multitude of nations" | Exact equivalence; πλῆθος = multitude, crowd; ἔθνη = nations, peoples |
| 17:11 | אוֹת בְּרִית ("sign of the covenant") | σημεῖον διαθήκης ("sign of the covenant/testament") | "sign of the covenant" | Exact equivalence; LXX uses διαθήκη (diatheke) consistently for בְּרִית — the word that gives us the NT concept of the "new covenant/testament" |
| 17:14 | נִכְרְתָה הַנֶּפֶשׁ הַהִוא ("that person shall be cut off") | ἐξολεθρευθήσεται ἡ ψυχὴ ἐκείνη ("that soul shall be utterly destroyed") | "shall be cut off from his people" | LXX's ἐξολεθρεύω (exolethreuo, "to destroy completely, to exterminate") is stronger than MT's karet; implies death rather than excommunication |
| 17:17 | וַיִּצְחָק ("and he laughed") | καὶ ἐγέλασεν ("and he laughed") | "laughed" | Exact equivalence; both texts unambiguously record the laughter |
| 17:18 | לוּ יִשְׁמָעֵאל יִחְיֶה לְפָנֶיךָ ("Oh that Ishmael might live before you") | εἴθε Ισμαηλ ζήσεται ἐναντίον σου ("Would that Ishmael might live before you") | "Oh that Ishmael might live before you" | Exact equivalence; εἴθε (eithe) = "would that, if only" — the same optative wish-particle as לוּ |
| 17:19 | יִצְחָק ("Isaac") | Ισαακ ("Isaac") | "Isaac" | Transliteration in both; the laughter etymology is embedded in the Hebrew form |
| 17:20 | שְׁנֵים עָשָׂר נְשִׂיאִם ("twelve princes/chiefs") | δώδεκα ἔθνη ("twelve nations") | "twelve princes" | LXX renders נְשִׂיאִם (nesi'im, "princes, chiefs") as ἔθνη (ethne, "nations") — a significant shift from leaders to peoples; MT's "princes" is more precise |

The most significant LXX variant is at verse 1, where the Vaticanus renders אֵל שַׁדַּי simply as ὁ θεός σου ("your God"), omitting any attempt to render Shaddai. This is a departure from other LXX witnesses, which sometimes render Shaddai as παντοκράτωρ (pantokrator, "Almighty"). The Vaticanus's substitution may reflect a theological tendency to avoid the distinct patriarchal divine name, or a manuscript tradition at this point that differs from the MT's name.

The variant at verse 20 is also significant: the LXX renders נְשִׂיאִם (nesi'im, "princes/leaders/chiefs") as ἔθνη (ethne, "nations/peoples") — transforming the twelve tribal leaders who will come from Ishmael into twelve nations. The MT's "twelve princes" (confirmed by Genesis 25:12–18, which names the twelve sons of Ishmael as the twelve princes of their people) is more precise and historically specific; the LXX's rendering generalizes the promise into a promise of twelve peoples.

---

### Structural and Theological Notes

**The Architecture of Genesis 17 — Covenant Structure and Chiasm**

Genesis 17 is the most formally constructed covenant text in the Abraham narrative. It consists of a divine speech (vv. 1–21) followed by a narrative response (vv. 22–27). The divine speech has a careful structure:

**A — The Divine Self-Introduction and Call (vv. 1–2):** El Shaddai appears; commands walking and blamelessness; announces the covenant.

**B — Abraham's Prostration (v. 3a):** The human response of worship.

**C — The Covenant of Fruitfulness: Abram → Abraham (vv. 3b–8):** God announces Abraham will be father of a multitude of nations; the name change; the promise of the land for an everlasting possession; the covenant formula "I will be their God."

**D — The Sign: Circumcision (vv. 9–14):** The covenant obligation imposed on Abraham and his household; the sign of circumcision; the karet penalty for non-compliance.

**C' — The Covenant of Fruitfulness: Sarai → Sarah (vv. 15–16):** The parallel name change; the promise of a son through Sarah; Sarah as mother of nations and kings.

**B' — Abraham's Prostration and Laughter (v. 17):** The human response — this time including laughter.

**A' — The Covenant Particularized: Abraham's Plea and God's Answer (vv. 18–21):** Abraham intercedes for Ishmael; God confirms the covenant runs through Isaac; Ishmael receives a blessing but not the covenant.

**Narrative Conclusion (vv. 22–27):** God departs; Abraham immediately performs circumcision on all males of his household.

The structural symmetry highlights the covenant's scope: it encompasses both male (Abraham) and female (Sarah) partners; both the chosen line (Isaac) and the non-chosen line (Ishmael) receive divine attention; the sign (circumcision) marks the transition between the C and C' sections.

**Genesis 17 and the Abrahamic Covenant — Three Covenant Moments**

The Abrahamic covenant is disclosed in three escalating moments in Genesis:

1. **Genesis 12:1–3** — The initial call and promise: land, offspring, blessing. Unconditional, with no sign.

2. **Genesis 15** — The covenant formalized: Abram believes; God passes through the animal pieces; the covenant is "cut" (karat) in the most solemn ancient form. The offspring and land promises are reiterated. The sign: the covenant-cutting ceremony itself. God alone passes through.

3. **Genesis 17** — The covenant expanded and signed in flesh: the name changes (Abram → Abraham; Sarai → Sarah); the fruitfulness promise is made explicit in terms of nations and kings; the covenant is declared "everlasting" (olam) for the first time with respect to both offspring and land; the sign of circumcision is instituted; and the particularization to Isaac's line is announced for the first time.

The three covenant texts are not repetitions or contradictions but a progressive disclosure. Genesis 17 adds what Genesis 12 and 15 did not include: the sign in flesh, the name changes as covenant markers, the identification of Sarah as the covenant mother, and the explicit distinction between the Isaanic covenant line and the Ishmaelite blessed-but-non-covenant line.

**El Shaddai, Thirteen Years, and the Silence of Genesis 16–17**

Between Genesis 16 (the birth of Ishmael, when Abram was 86 years old) and Genesis 17 (when Abraham is 99 years old) there is a gap of thirteen years with no recorded divine speech. The text moves from "Abram was eighty-six years old when Hagar bore Ishmael to Abram" (Genesis 16:16) directly to "when Abram was ninety-nine years old, the LORD appeared to Abram." Thirteen years of silence — the longest gap in the Abraham narrative. The ancient interpreters (from the rabbinic tradition to Origen and beyond) noticed this silence. Was God silent because Abram was raising Ishmael as the heir? Was the silence a period of divine waiting? The text does not say. But the silence gives the opening of Genesis 17 its drama: after thirteen years, El Shaddai speaks, and everything changes.

**The Covenant Formula — אֱלֹהִים לָהֶם — "I Will Be Their God" (v. 8)**

The covenant formula in verse 8 — לִהְיוֹת לְךָ לֵאלֹהִים וּלְזַרְעֲךָ אַחֲרֶיךָ (lihyot lekha le'elohim ulezar'akha acharekha, "to be God to you and to your offspring after you") — is one of the most foundational formulas in the entire biblical covenant tradition. It recurs throughout the Pentateuch and the Prophets in various forms:

- Exodus 6:7: "I will take you to be my people, and I will be your God."
- Leviticus 26:12: "I will walk among you and will be your God, and you shall be my people."
- Jeremiah 31:33: "I will be their God, and they shall be my people."
- Ezekiel 36:28: "You shall be my people, and I will be your God."
- Revelation 21:3: "He will dwell with them, and they will be his people, and God himself will be with them as their God."

The formula is bilateral — a mutual belonging — even though the initiation is entirely divine. God will be their God; the implicit counterpart is that they will be his people. The formal translation renders "to be God to you and to your offspring after you" — following the Hebrew lihyot le'elohim ("to be as God, to be God to") with the preposition le- indicating relational identification.

**Isaac and the Name of Laughter**

God's instruction to name the child יִצְחָק (Yitschaq, "Isaac") in verse 19 carries the entire weight of the laughter of verse 17. The child who will be born through biological impossibility will carry in his name the laughter that greeted his announcement. But in the biblical narrative the laughter is not mocking — it becomes the child's identity. Isaac's name is the crystallized memory of the moment when his parents found the promise of his birth too good to be true.

The formal translation renders "you shall call his name Isaac" — following the Hebrew construction אֲשֶׁר תִּקְרָא אֶת שְׁמוֹ יִצְחָק precisely. The covenant with Isaac (v. 19) is then specified as "an everlasting covenant for his offspring after him" — applying the same olam ("everlasting") qualifier to the Isaanic line that was applied to the broader Abrahamic covenant in verse 7.

**Ishmael and the Twelve Princes**

God's response to Abraham's prayer for Ishmael (v. 20) is notable for its generosity: הִנֵּה בֵּרַכְתִּי אֹתוֹ וְהִפְרֵיתִי אֹתוֹ וְהִרְבֵּיתִי אֹתוֹ בִּמְאֹד מְאֹד ("Behold, I will bless him and make him fruitful and multiply him exceedingly" — the same fruitfulness language as the covenant promise to Abraham himself, with the double מְאֹד מְאֹד, "exceedingly" × 2, as an intensifier). Ishmael will be the father of twelve נְשִׂיאִם (nesi'im, "princes/chiefs") and a great nation (גּוֹי גָּדוֹל, goy gadol). The LXX renders these princes as ἔθνη (ethne, "nations") — the formal translation follows the MT's nesi'im as "princes," since Genesis 25:12–18 names all twelve individually and calls them נְשִׂיאִים (princes/chiefs), not twelve nations.

The key distinction is stated explicitly in verse 21: "But my covenant I will establish with Isaac" — וְאֶת בְּרִיתִי אָקִים אֶת יִצְחָק (ve'et beriti aqim et Yitschaq). Ishmael receives blessing; Isaac receives the covenant. The distinction is between general divine beneficence (Ishmael) and specific covenant relationship (Isaac). Both are sons of Abraham; both are loved; only one carries the redemptive-historical covenant.

---

— LOGOS
